title: Buddha in the Attitude of Meditation
story: This artifact, Buddha in the Attitude of Meditation, originates from Jaulian and dates back to 4th Century A.D. – 5th Century A.D.. It is associated with Buddhism and made of stucco and stone.

title: Buddha Seated in the Attitude of Meditation, With Other Attendants.
story:
This sculpture depicts the Buddha seated in meditation, with his hands placed in the Dhyana Mudra, a gesture traditionally associated with deep concentration, inner balance, and spiritual enlightenment in Buddhist art. The hands resting in the lap symbolize the unity of wisdom and compassion, reinforcing the meditative focus of the central figure.
At the right rear stands Vajrapani, identified in Buddhist tradition as a protective bodhisattva and guardian of the Buddha. In Gandharan iconography, Vajrapani often appears as a powerful attendant figure, visually emphasizing protection and spiritual strength surrounding the Buddha.
The presence of a fly-whisk (chauri) bearer further elevates the scene. In South Asian artistic conventions, the fly-whisk is a symbol of honor and reverence, typically reserved for royalty or highly venerated spiritual figures. Its inclusion highlights the Buddha’s exalted status while framing the composition with attendants who signify devotion, protection, and sacred authority.

title:
Buddha in the Abstinence Pose, The Man With the Pointed Cap
is Probably the Donor Along With His Wife on His Left.
story:
This sculpture shows the Buddha in a simple and serious pose, connected to a life of self-discipline and renunciation. The folds of his robe are carved in a detailed style that is typical of Gandharan art from the 4th–5th century A.D.
A man wearing a pointed cap is shown near the Buddha. The plaque says he is probably the donor, meaning he likely gave money or support to help build or decorate the monastery. A smaller figure beside him is identified as his wife. In Gandharan Buddhist art, donors were often shown in smaller size to show respect, while the Buddha is made larger to show his spiritual importance.
The difference in size between the Buddha and the other figures helps show who is most important in the scene.

title: Figure of Buddha in the Attitude of Preaching and Meditation
story:
This sculpture shows the Buddha sitting calmly inside an arched niche. His hands are raised in front of his chest in the teaching gesture which is Dharmachakra Mudra. This hand position represents the Buddha giving his first sermon and sharing his teachings. His seated posture also shows meditation and inner peace.
Around the Buddha are several attendants standing close to him. These figures are smaller, which shows respect and highlights the Buddha as the most important figure in the scene. The round halo behind his head is a symbol of enlightenment and spiritual light.
The detailed folds of the robe and the arched frame are typical features of Gandharan art from the 5th–6th century A.D., found at the Buddhist site of Mohra Moradu near Taxila.

title: Stupa Shaped Relic Casket
story:
This object is a stupa-shaped relic casket from Jaulian, dating from the 2nd to 4th century A.D. A relic casket was used to keep sacred remains or important religious objects connected to the Buddha or respected monks.
Its shape copies a stupa, which is a Buddhist monument built to honor the Buddha and hold relics. The layered, rising design at the top represents the traditional stupa form, which symbolizes spiritual growth and the path toward enlightenment.
The middle part is decorated with small stones and ornaments. Such decoration shows that the object was special and treated with respec

title: Earliest Stucco Sculpture
story: This collection presents fragmentary sculpted heads from the Gandhara region, once part of larger Buddhist devotional and architectural settings. Despite their incomplete state, the faces retain distinctive expressions, hairstyles, and stylistic features that reflect the cultural exchange and refined craftsmanship characteristic of Gandharan art. Together, they offer insight into early sculptural traditions and the spiritual aesthetics of the period

title: Head of Bodhistava with Turbans
story: This head of a Bodhisattva is distinguished by its elaborately wrapped turban, a feature associated with princely adornment and spiritual nobility. The calm expression and softly modeled features reflect the contemplative ideal central to Gandharan Buddhist imagery. Though fragmentary, the sculpture conveys both refinement and quiet authority, embodying the devotional and artistic traditions of its time.

title: Buddha in Meditation
story: This statue shows the Buddha seated in meditation, with a calm and peaceful expression. His hands rest gently in his lap, one placed over the other, with the thumbs lightly touching. This hand position represents deep thought and inner peace. The simple folds of his robe and his relaxed posture add to the quiet and serene feeling of the sculpture.

title: Death Of Buddha
story: The central scene depicts the Buddha reclining on his right side, peacefully resting on a cushioned support, representing the moment of his Parinirvana which is his final passing and release from the cycle of rebirth. Surrounding him are attendants and disciples shown in varied emotional responses, with some figures expressing grief while others appear composed. Such narrative relief panels were typically installed on the exterior of stupas, where they illustrated important events from the Buddha’s life and teachings.

title: Stucco Sculptures
story: The sculptures exhibit stylistic characteristics consistent with Gandharan Buddhist art, which flourished in regions including Taxila between the 3rd century BCE and 4th century CE. However, without provenance documentation or archaeological records, the exact origin cannot be conclusively verified from visual analysis alone.

title: Buddha in Meditation
story:
This sculpture shows Buddha sitting in meditation. He is sitting cross-legged with his hands placed in his lap. This hand position is called the Dhyana Mudra, which means meditation and deep concentration.
There is a round circle (halo) behind his head. The halo shows that he is enlightened and holy.
On both sides of Buddha, there are two smaller figures carved in the background. These are likely followers or helpers (called Bodhisattvas), but parts of them are damaged.
The sculpture is made of stone and shows detailed folds in the clothing. The facial expression of Buddha looks calm and peaceful.

title: Bodhisattva Under an Arch
story:
This stone plaque shows a Bodhisattva seated inside an arched niche. He wears jewelry and a decorated headdress, which shows he is a Bodhisattva (not Buddha).
You can see the crown on his head, necklace around his neck, and his hands placed together in front, while the arch frames him like a doorway.

title: Buddha Head
story: This shows head of Buddha from Jaulian.

title: Fragments of Votive Stupa from the Dharamarajika Stupa
story:
The images showcase fragments of a votive stupa from the Dharmarajika Stupa, which was a significant Buddhist monument in ancient times. These artifacts, likely part of a larger stupa, feature intricate carvings depicting scenes related to Buddhist themes, such as figures seated in meditation or possibly representations of the Buddha. The votive stupa is a type of Buddhist structure often built to commemorate important events, relics, or figures associated with Buddhism.
The carvings on the fragments display detailed reliefs, with scenes of figures positioned in meditative or ritualistic poses, possibly indicating depictions of the Buddha or other revered figures in Buddhist traditions. The decorative elements along the top of the stupa's base, which feature ornate motifs, suggest the high level of craftsmanship during the period in which it was made.

title: A Section of the Plinth Railing Harmika and Umberellas Excavated from Sirkap
story: This artifact from Sirkap is a remarkable example of Buddhist architecture . The plinth railing which is base structure that surrounds a stupa, harmika, and umbrellas represent the rich symbolism and spiritual significance of stupas in Gandharan Buddhist culture, highlighting the cosmic symbolism of Buddhist architecture and its connection to spiritual enlightenment. The influence of Hellenistic art and Greek culture, fused with Buddhist themes, is a defining feature of this artifact, showcasing the cultural confluence of the region during this period.

title: Buddha Statue
story: The depiction of the Buddha in a naturalistic style with flowing robes, a halo, and finely detailed features is typical of this period, emphasizing the Buddha’s enlightenment and spiritual presence.

title: Buddha in the Attitude of Teaching with other Bodhisattvas and Devas
story: This image shows a carved stone relief panel from the Gandhara Buddhist art tradition, depicting the Buddha in the teaching posture (likely the Dharmachakra mudra) surrounded by bodhisattvas, devas, and attendants. The relief appears to be arranged in two narrative tiers: the upper register shows standing figures in a devotional or ceremonial setting, while the lower register presents a more detailed narrative scene framed by architectural columns, which is typical of Gandharan storytelling panels.

title: Death Of Buddha (Confiscated)
story: It depicts the Parinirvana, or the final passing of Siddhartha Gautama into Nirvana, marking his release from the cycle of rebirth. The Buddha is shown reclining on his right side upon an elevated couch, a standard iconographic posture for this scene. He is surrounded by a hierarchy of figures: the monk Subhadra sits in calm meditation at the front, contrasted against mourning lay and monastic followers who express deep grief, such as his disciple Ananda.

title: Buddha with his disciples
story: This image shows a small Gandharan stone relief depicting the Buddha seated on a pedestal, surrounded by attendant figures, likely bodhisattvas or disciples. The Buddha appears to display the Dharmachakra mudra (gesture of teaching), with hands positioned near the chest symbolizing the turning of the wheel of Dharma and the act of delivering teachings.

title: Buddha and Female Devotee in Niches
story: This narrative relief panel features a pair of arched architectural niches separated by a central Corinthian-style column. The right niche contains a standing Buddha in the abhaya mudra (gesture of reassurance), while the left niche depicts a high-status female figure, likely a royal devotee or donor, rendered with intricate Hellenistic drapery. Notably, two sacred birds (possibly hansas or parrots) are perched atop the central architectural junction, serving as auspicious symbols of spiritual transcendence.

title: False Dormer Fragment Depicting Buddhist Scenes
story: The relief is arranged in three narrative registers. In the top register, a seated Buddha figure,  is shown flanked by worshippers within a pointed archway. The middle register presents a row of several standing or seated figures, likely representing celestial beings or devoted attendants participating in a sacred gathering. The bottom register contains the primary narrative scene, depicting a central seated Buddha surrounded by larger kneeling or standing attendants shown in deep reverence, emphasizing the hierarchical and devotional structure typical of Gandharan narrative compositions.

title: Seated Buddha with Worshippers
story: A Gandharan stone relief depicting a seated Buddha in teaching posture surrounded by worshippers and attendants within architectural niches, reflecting devotional narrative scenes typical of the Dharmarajika tradition.

title: Gandharan Narrative Frieze with Attendants and Devotees
story: A Gandharan narrative relief showing multiple standing figures arranged in two registers within architectural framing, likely depicting devotees or celestial attendants participating in a sacred Buddhist scene.

title: Renunciation of Sidhartha
story:
This Gandharan schist relief panel illustrates the Renunciation of Prince Siddhartha (the future Buddha) through a continuous narrative composition divided by a central Corinthian column.
On the left side, Siddhartha is depicted riding a horse and accompanied by attendants as he departs from a populated architectural setting. The figures walking beside the horse suggest guidance and escort during his departure from royal life.

title: Renunciation of Sidhartha
story: The image of the hut is a potent symbol in human imagination, occupying a considerable segment of architectural history and imagination, its presence being more powerful as ideas than as actual or practical structures. In most ideational treatment of the hut -- the hut as primitive, originary, paradisal, archetypal or regenerative -- it has been pursued as an elemental dwelling. Included in this genre is also the hermit's hut, the actual or the ideational structure that belongs to a hermit, recluse or ascetic. The dissertation explores the hermit's hut as a key trope in ascetic discourse and its significance in cultural and architectural imagination.

title: Renunciation of Sidhartha
story: Seated in the center on a high, cushioned pedestal adorned with floral motifs, the Buddha is the largest figure to emphasize his importance. He is shown in a meditative posture, often with a plain circular nimbus (halo) behind his head. The Buddha is flanked by smaller figures of devotees or worshippers, some standing and some appearing as half-figures.

title: Renunciation of Sidhartha
story:
The central figure is Siddhartha mounted on his loyal horse, Kanthaka.
He is surrounded by attendants, including his charioteer Chandaka, and celestial beings who celebrate his decision.
A key detail in this narrative is the presence of yakshas (earth spirits) shown crouching beneath the horse's hooves. According to tradition, they lifted the hooves to ensure Kanthaka made no sound, preventing the palace guards and Siddhartha's family from waking.

title: Renunciation of Sidhartha
story: This Gandharan schist relief fragment depicts a scene from the life of the Buddha, specifically related to his path toward enlightenment and his role as a teacher within the ancient Gandhara region

title: Bodhisattva Maitreya
story:
Unlike the historical Buddha who is often shown in simple monastic robes, Maitreya is depicted as a Bodhisattva, wearing the turban, elaborate jewels, and princely attire associated with the nobility of the Kushan Empire.
In his left hand, he holds a ritual water pot (often shaped like a lotus bud), a characteristic symbol of Maitreya in Gandharan art. His right hand is raised in the Abhaya Mudra, representing the dispelling of fear or a gesture of protection.
His spiritual status is marked by the halo (nimbus) behind his head and the ushnisha (topknot of hair) on his head

title: Buddha with Worshippers
story: Panels like this often illustrate scenes from the life of the Buddha. In this specific fragment, figures are shown in superimposed compartments, flanked by decorative borders.

title: Buddha with Worshippers
story: The carving shows a devotee in a posture of adoration (anjali mudra) standing next to a ceremonial pillar. Such pillars were common decorative and symbolic elements in Gandharan architectural panels.

title: Buddha with Worshippers
story: The right hand is raised near the chest or shoulder, which is characteristic of the Abhaya Mudra (gesture of fearlessness and protection). The left hand resting on the hip is a classic Hellenistic (Greek) artistic influence frequently seen in Gandharan sculpture. This "hand-on-hip" stance was used to denote authority.

title: Buddha with Worshippers
story: The figure stands within a chaitya arch (a horseshoe-shaped niche), has both palms pressed together in front of the chest, which is the defining posture of the Anjali Mudra denotes respect, devotion, and prayer..

title: Buddha with Worshippers
story: a standing Buddha,  raises his right hand in Abhaya mudra, symbolizing reassurance and protection, surrounded by attendants or devotees.

title: Buddha with Worshippers
story: Grey schist Gandhāran relief depicting three stacked scenes from the life of the Buddha. The lower register shows the Buddha beneath a tree, likely representing Enlightenment. The middle register presents a seated teaching Buddha, and the upper register shows a standing Buddha in Abhaya mudra.

title: Buddha, Bodhisattavas and Worshippers
story: Buddha Shakyamuni, seated on a throne beneath a stylized Bodhi tree (the pipal tree of enlightenment). He is flanked by Bodhisattvas (identifiable by their elaborate turbans, jewelry, and princely attire) and worshippers in poses of devotion.

title: Buddha, Bodhisattavas and Worshippers
story: depicting Buddha Great Departure, during which Prince Siddhartha is shown leaving his palace riding the white horse Kanthaka. The Buddha is here portrayed richly adorned, riding his horse, surrounded by servants and worshippers

title: Buddha with Worshippers
story: It shows Buddha with Worshippers. Buddha's right hand is raised to shoulder level with the palm facing outward. This gesture signifies fearlessness, protection, and reassurance to his followers

title: Kuvera/ Jambala
story: This sculpture represents Kuvera (also known as Kubera), the god of wealth and king of the yakshas in Hindu tradition, who is equated with the Buddhist deity of wealth, Jambala

title: Kuvera/ Jambala
story: A devotional or encounter scene involving a central monastic figure (possibly the Buddha) receiving or addressing lay devotees.

title: Buddha Figure
story: He is seated, possibly in a meditative posture common in Gandharan depictions of enlightened beings in contemplation.

title: Buddha Figure
story: the figure features a large, circular halo representing divinity and a prominent ushnisha (topknot) symbolizing spiritual wisdom. The sculpture's heavy, naturalistic drapery with deep vertical folds reflects Roman influence, while the serene facial expression and wavy hair are modeled after classical Greek aesthetics. The right hand is raised in the Abhaya Mudra, a gesture of reassurance and protection, and the presence of ornate jewelry like the beaded necklace signifies his role as an enlightened being who remains connected to the world to assist others

title: Buddha with Devotees
story: The Buddha is depicted seated in a meditative posture, often referred to as dhyanasana or padmasana. He is framed by a plain rectangular border or niche. He is flanked by two standing figures, likely devotees, attendants, or celestial beings

title: Buddha Figure
story: Buddha is shown in the Dhyana Mudra, with hands resting in his lap to symbolize deep spiritual concentration. The piece demonstrates a masterful fusion of cultures, combining Buddhist iconography, such as the ushnisha  which is wisdom topknot and urna also known as spiritual third eye. Framed by a large, plain nimbus (halo) that signifies his enlightenment. The small figures carved into the Buddha's halo  are typically identified as Bodhisattvas or high-ranking deities who serve as his attendants

title: Buddha Figure
story: It features a large, plain halo (nimbus) behind the head, which signifies enlightenment and divine status. The Buddha's hands are held in the Dharmachakra mudra, representing the "turning of the wheel of the doctrine" or preaching.

title: Buddha Figure
story: It features a large, plain halo (nimbus) behind the head, which signifies enlightenment and divine status. The Buddha's hands are held in the Abhaya Mudra, the gesture of fearlessness, protection, and peace.

title: Bodhisattva
story:
The small vessel held in his left hand is the primary iconographic symbol for Maitreya. It represents the nectar of wisdom and his role as a future savior.
 Unlike the historical Buddha, who is usually shown in simple monk's robes, Bodhisattvas are depicted as princes. He wears elaborate jewelry, including heavy necklaces, armlets, and earrings, signifying his status before total renunciation.

title: Bodhisattva Maitreya
story:
Bodhisattva Maitreya: He is identified as the Future Buddha, an enlightened being currently waiting in the Tushita heaven to be reborn on Earth.
Although his left hand is damaged, he originally held a small water vessel, which is his primary symbol representing the nectar of wisdom and life.
Unlike the simple monastic robes of the historical Buddha, Maitreya is depicted as a prince wearing elaborate jewelry, including a heavy torque, long necklaces with amulet boxes, and ornate armbands.

title: Un Named
story: The relief shows the central figure of the Buddha interacting with devotees or monks

title: schist false gable
story: These intricate panels served as the summit or centerpiece of a stupa, acting as a false gable for structural or decorative purposes. The vertical arrangement of panels allowed for the telling of a continuous narrative, starting from the bottom and moving upwards to follow the Buddha's spiritual journey.

title: Buddha Seated in the Attitude of Meditation
story: Buddha is Seated in the Attitude of Meditation

title: Buddha Seated in the Attitude of Meditation
story: Buddha is Seated in the Attitude of Meditation

title: Bodhisattva
story:
The topknot of hair and the forehead mark, signifying spiritual wisdom and insight.
Halo (Nimbus): A circular disk behind the head representing the Buddha's divine status.

title: Bodhisattva
story:
Unlike the simple, monastic look of the historical Buddha, Bodhisattvas are depicted as princely figures wearing elaborate jewelry, including heavy beaded necklaces and armlets.
Divine Symbols: The figure features a large, plain circular halo (nimbus), an ushnisha (spiritual topknot), and an urna (third-eye mark on the forehead).

title: Bodhisattva
story:
Unlike the simple, monastic look of the historical Buddha, Bodhisattvas are depicted as princely figures wearing elaborate jewelry, including heavy beaded necklaces and armlets.
Divine Symbols: The figure features a large, plain circular halo (nimbus), an ushnisha (spiritual topknot), and an urna (third-eye mark on the forehead).

title: Bodhisattva
story:
Unlike the simple, monastic look of the historical Buddha, Bodhisattvas are depicted as princely figures wearing elaborate jewelry, including heavy beaded necklaces and armlets.
Divine Symbols: The figure features a large, plain circular halo (nimbus), an ushnisha (spiritual topknot), and an urna (third-eye mark on the forehead).

title: Bodhisattva
story:
Unlike the simple, monastic look of the historical Buddha, Bodhisattvas are depicted as princely figures wearing elaborate jewelry, including heavy beaded necklaces and armlets.
Divine Symbols: The figure features a large, plain circular halo (nimbus), an ushnisha (spiritual topknot), and an urna (third-eye mark on the forehead).

title: Buddha in attitude of preaching
story: Buddha in attitude of preaching under an arch

title: un Named
story: The fragment depicts a group of figures in dynamic, interactive poses, which is characteristic of the prolific iconography used to tell stories of the Buddha's current and previous lives

title: Atlas
story: It exemplifies the blending of Greek mythology and Buddhist symbolism. It depicts the Greek Titan Atlas, often depicted with wings in this context, bearing the weight of the heavens.

title: un Named
story: The panel is divided into four arched niches by pillars. Each niche contains a figure, likely illustrating specific events or devotees in the life of the Buddha.

title: Buddha In Meditation
story: The relief depicts the Buddha in Meditation (Dhyanasana), with his hands joined in his lap in the Dhyana Mudra. He is flanked by two standing figures, likely devotees or attendants in a pose of adoration.

title: un Named
story:
A continuous row of figures, likely worshippers or attendants, depicted in dynamic poses. Some similar friezes show these figures with hands folded in a gesture of adoration.
 A prominent checkerboard or lattice pattern, which in Gandharan architecture often represents a vedika or sacred railing used to demarcate a holy space.

title: Buddha Preaching
story: Buddha standing on a raised platform within an architectural niche, surrounded by attendants or devotees holding offerings. The Buddha’s right hand appears positioned in a gesture consistent with a teaching or reassurance mudra, though weathering makes the exact gesture uncertain.

title: Buddha with Vijrapani and other lay worshippers (Confiscated)
story: Buddha with Vijrapani and other lay worshippers

title: Buddha with Vijrapani and other lay worshippers (Confiscated)
story: This fragmentary relief shows two mounted figures, one on a horse and another on a camel, suggesting a travel or processional scene. The riders are dressed in draped garments, and the animals are rendered with naturalistic detail typical of Gandharan narrative sculpture. Such imagery often appears in episodes depicting journeys, royal processions, or the movement of devotees within larger narrative cycle

title: A gray schist stupa relief with garland bearers
story: The relief depicts an undulating garland supported by amorini (small, winged male figure also known as putti or genii).

title: Buddha with Vijrapani and other lay worshippers (Confiscated)
story: This fragment features a standing, haloed figure—likely the Buddha—framed within an arched niche and flanked by Indo-Corinthian pilasters

title: Buddha with Vijrapani and other lay worshippers (Confiscated)
story: Buddha seated in a meditative posture preaching to worshippers

story: Shows Head of Lions from different architectural part

title: Buddhas Statue
story: features a prominent ushnisha (topknot) symbolizing enlightenment and is depicted in the Dharmachakra Mudra, the gesture of "turning the Wheel of Law," which signifies the Buddha's first sermon.

title: Cast of the Stupa
story: A plaster cast replica of a Buddhist stupa from the Mohra Moradu monastery, representing Gandharan architectural style from the Kushan period. The structure reflects the religious and monastic significance of stupas in early Buddhist practice.

title: Glass Bangles
story: A collection of glass bangles excavated from Bhir Mound, Sirkap, and various stupa sites in Taxila. These ornaments reflect the continuity of decorative traditions from the early historic period through the Kushan era, highlighting advancements in glass craftsmanship and everyday adornment practices.

extra.origin: Bhir Mound, Sirkap and Stupa Sites

title: Finger Rings of Lead
story: A collection of lead finger rings excavated from Bhir Mound, dating to the 3rd century B.C. These simple ornamental objects reflect early metal craftsmanship and everyday adornment practices during the Mauryan period in ancient Taxila.

title: Iron Cart with Spoked Wheels
story: An iron model cart featuring spoked wheels, excavated from the ancient city of Sirkap. Dating between the 2nd century B.C. and 2nd century A.D., the artifact reflects advancements in metal craftsmanship and provides insight into transportation and daily life during the Indo-Greek and subsequent periods in Taxila.

title: Stone Grinding Tools
story: A group of stone grinding tools, possibly including rotary querns and pestles, used for processing grains and spices. Such implements were commonly employed in domestic settings for food preparation in ancient Taxila.

title: Large Terracotta Storage Jars
story: A series of large terracotta storage jars displayed in the museum gallery. Such vessels were typically used for storing grains, water, oil, or other commodities in domestic and commercial settings in ancient Taxila. Their size suggests bulk storage purposes.

title: Glass Tiles
story: A collection of glass tiles excavated from the Dharmarajika Stupa complex at Taxila. These tiles were likely used as decorative architectural elements, reflecting the artistic and material sophistication of Buddhist monumental architecture in the Gandharan region.

title: Carved Brick Flooring
story: Carved terracotta bricks excavated from the Buddhist site of Bhamala in Taxila. These decorated flooring elements were likely used in monastic or stupa structures, reflecting the architectural and artistic traditions of the Gandharan region during the Kushan period.

title: Carved Stone Funerary Chest
story: A richly carved stone funerary chest from the Gandharan region, likely used to house urns or relic containers. The structure features architectural motifs and narrative relief carvings characteristic of Gandharan art during the Kushan period, reflecting funerary traditions and commemorative practices of the time.

title: Fragments of Votive Stupa from the Dharmarajika Stupa
story: Fragmentary remains of a votive stupa discovered at the Dharmarajika Stupa complex in Taxila. Votive stupas were small devotional monuments offered by pilgrims and devotees as acts of religious merit. These fragments reflect the flourishing Buddhist activity and artistic tradition of the Gandharan region.

title: Antiquities Recovered from Jinan Wali Dheri
story: A collection of antiquities excavated from the Buddhist site of Jinan Wali Dheri, dating from the 3rd to 8th century A.D. The assemblage includes ritual objects, architectural elements, ornaments, pottery, and beads, reflecting religious, domestic, and artistic practices of the Gandharan region.

extra.material: Mixed Materials (Copper, Iron, Silver, Stone, Terracotta)

title: Silver Utensils and Ornaments
story:
The Silver Utensils and Ornaments displayed in the museum represent the rich lifestyle, craftsmanship, and artistic culture of ancient Taxila civilization. These objects were used by wealthy families, nobles, and royal communities during the Gandhara period.

The collection usually includes:

Silver bowls and plates
Cups and containers
Jewelry items such as bracelets, rings, necklaces, and earrings
Decorative ornaments with engraved designs

These items show the advanced metalworking skills of ancient artisans. Craftsmen carefully shaped and decorated silver with detailed patterns, floral motifs, and sometimes religious symbols.

title: Silver Utensils
story: The Silver Utensils and Ornaments displayed in the museum represent the rich lifestyle, craftsmanship, and artistic culture of ancient Taxila civilization. These objects were used by wealthy families, nobles, and royal communities during the Gandhara period.

title: Death Of Buddha
story: Jewelry items such as bracelets, rings, necklaces, and earrings

title: Budha in the attitude of Reassurance
story: Budha in the attitude of Reassurance

title: Offering by Monkey
story:
The “Offering by Monkey” sculpture is a famous Buddhist artwork from the Gandhara civilization. It depicts a story from the life of Gautama Buddha in which a monkey respectfully offers food, usually honey or fruit, to Buddha.

In the sculpture, the monkey is shown approaching Buddha with devotion and humility. Buddha calmly accepts the offering, symbolizing kindness, compassion, and equality toward all living beings.

This scene represents an important Buddhist teaching:

Every act of kindness and devotion has value
Compassion extends to humans and animals alike
Even animals can show wisdom and respect
Artistic Features

The sculpture is an example of Gandhara art, known for:

Realistic body shapes and expressions
Detailed drapery in clothing
Greek-inspired artistic style
Calm and spiritual atmosphere
Historical Importance

The artwork reflects:

Buddhist moral teachings
The importance of generosity in Buddhism
The cultural and artistic excellence of ancient Taxila

It also shows how Buddhist stories were used in ancient art to teach moral lessons to people.

title: Devadattas Assassin
story:
The “Devadatta’s Assassin” sculpture represents a story from the life of Gautama Buddha. According to Buddhist tradition, Devadatta, who was Buddha’s jealous cousin and rival, tried several times to harm him. In this scene, Devadatta sends assassins to kill Buddha.

However, when the assassins meet Buddha, they are deeply influenced by his peaceful personality, wisdom, and compassion. Instead of attacking him, they abandon violence and become respectful followers.

Meaning and Symbolism

The sculpture teaches important Buddhist values:

Peace is stronger than violence
Compassion can change human behavior
Wisdom defeats hatred and jealousy

The scene also highlights Buddha’s calm nature and spiritual power.

Artistic Features

This artwork is part of the Gandhara art tradition and often includes:

Realistic human figures
Detailed facial expressions
Greek-style clothing folds and sculpture techniques
Dramatic storytelling through art
Historical Importance

The sculpture reflects:

Buddhist religious stories used for moral teaching
The artistic excellence of ancient Taxila civilization
The influence of Gandhara culture in Buddhist art

title: Offering Of Four Babies
story: The “Offering of Four Babies” sculpture in Taxila Museum represents devotion and the seeking of blessings for children. It symbolizes purity, compassion, and faith in Buddhist teachings. The artwork is an important example of Gandhara art and ancient Buddhist culture.
